This chart shows how my sleep varies throughout the week and helps identify patterns that may impact my productivity. Highest Sleep: Sunday, Tuesday, Friday (~7.5-8 hrs) Likely due to no early morning classes/work. Lower Sleep on other days (~6-6.5 hrs) Caused by early morning classes + late night commitments Connection to Hypothesis: Shows when I am more vs less well-rested I can compare this to next-day study duration. Expectation: More Sleep → Better focus/productivity → This graph by itself is not enough to confirm or reject my hypothesis, but it provides useful context for further analysis (done in the next slides)

Does More Sleep Reduce Stress? | This graph compares the trends in my sleep duration and study duration across the data collection period to see if there is a relationship between the two. Some days show a similar pattern in the declining/increasing trend (higher hours of sleep → higher study time) However, the relationship is not consistent across all days. There are days where: I had high sleep but lower study time Or lower sleep but still studied a lot Study time appears to be also influenced by other factors (schedule, workload, motivation, midterms season) Conclusion on Hypothesis: The data does not consistently show that more sleep leads to more study time. As seen in the chart, there are some days where the relationship appears weak and inconsistent. Therefore, I reject my hypothesis based on this data from this line plot.

Relationship between Sleep Duration and Study Duration : Does More Sleep Lead to Longer Study Durations? | This scatter plot shows a slight positive relationship between sleep duration and study duration, meaning that in some cases, more sleep is associated with more study time However, the data points are kind of spread out, indicating that the relationship is not very strong or consistent. Overall, there is a small upward trend. But again, other factors are likely to influence the study duration like my environment, who I’m studying with, and my motivation. Conclusion on Hypothesis: The scatter plot shows a slight positive trend (higher sleep sometimes aligns with higher study time). However, the points are fairly spread out with no clear pattern. Therefore, I do not fully confirm the hypothesis, BUT there is some weak evidence supporting it as there is a slight positive trend.

Does Phone Use Before Bed Affect Sleep? | This scatter plot includes my screen time before bed in hours and sleep duration in hours. The scatter plot shows a weak negative relationship between screen time before bed and sleep duration. In general, as screen time before bed increases, sleep duration seems to slightly decrease. This means screen time may be an important factor that affects how much sleep someone gets. Connection to My Hypothesis: My hypothesis states that more sleep is linked to better focused study time the next day. This graph helps show why screen time before bed should be controlled in the analysis. If screen time affects sleep duration, it could also influence my study focus indirectly. Conclusion: This plot does not directly prove or reject my hypothesis. However, it supports using screen time before bed as a controlled variable when testing the relationship between sleep and focused study time.

This week, I decided to analyze my screen time habits to see if that affected my sleep duration and how my sleep duration may affect my napping habits. From the blue scatter plot, it looks like there isn’t a very strong relationship between how long I nap during the day and how much sleep I got the night before. Most of the points where the nap duration is 0 minutes (I didn’t nap that day) still show different amounts of sleep, ranging from around 7 to almost 9 hours of sleep. On the other hand, even when the nap duration increases to around 20-100 minutes, the sleep duration from the night before still stays around the 6-7 hour range. So, based on this graph, it doesn’t seem like longer naps are connected to getting less or more sleep the night before. The points are pretty spread out rather than forming a clear upward or downward trend shape, which suggests there isn’t a strong relationship between the two variables in this small sample. From this week’s data, it seems my napping habits may be influenced by other factors such as how tired I feel during the day or how busy my schedule of class is rather than just how much I slept the night before. | From the red scatter plot, it also does not look like there is a strong relationship between screen time before bed and how long I slept that night. The points are somewhat spread out. For example, on nights when my screen time was around 30 minutes, my sleep duration had a varied range of data (from around 6.5 hours to almost 9 hours). Even when screen time increased to around 45 or 60 minutes, the sleep duration did not consistently decrease. This suggests that, well at least from this week’s data, screen time alone may not be the main factor affecting how long I sleep. Overall, the graph does not show a clear positive or negative correlation. I think maybe my stress level, workload, or what time I actually went to bed might have a bigger impact on my sleep duration. For next week (the last week of data gathering), I could probably try take note of what apps I’m using before bed to see if that’ll affect my sleep duration.

I was curious to see if there was any correlation between my study duration and caffeine intake, like if I was more caffeinated did it cause me to “lock in” and study longer, or would it cause me to become more energized and distractible and jump from task to task. There is a VERY weak negative correlation between caffeine intake and study duration, implying that maybe more caffeine while studying doesn't always lead to longer study time. I would like to next see if it impacts my distractibility because I feel like it could make me more focused, or it could make me jittery and distractible. Maybe there’s a “sweet spot.” | Disclaimer: used chat gpt to add the trendline and r value to the “Total Caffeine vs Study Minutes” chart only

From this week’s data, I learned that my stress level seems to be connected to both my sleep and my study habits. On days when I slept more (around 7.5-8 hrs), my stress level was lower (around a score of 1). For stress, I said 1 was low stress and 5 was high stress level. However, when my sleep dropped to around 6-6.5 hours, my stress increased to level 3-4. I also noticed that when I studied fewer hours, my stress was sometimes higher especially in the middle of the week. This could be because I have my more stressful, challenging classes and my internship in the middle of the week. This suggests that both sleep and how much I get down academically affect how stressed I feel. | I tried to sleep more this week but I had to stay up late to focus on my capstone project for my IS436 class and I had a midterm in the middle of the week. So, next week, I will try again and aim for at least 7.5 hours of sleep and stay consistent with that because it seems my lowest stress levels occur on days I get at least 7 hours of sleep. I also want to spread out my studying more evenly instead of letting my productivity drop too low on certain days, since that could be contributing to the days where my stress is higher. | One barrier I experienced this time was deciding how to visually represent the data. I wasn’t sure whether to use bar graphs or line graphs or just combine them. Especially because stress was being compared to two different variables (sleep hours and study hours). Again, it’s kind of difficult to interpret the data using data on week at at time. But, overall, it seems my data variables are having a positive relationship with each other.

I spend most of my time studying sociology (due to it being a hybrid course) and data science (due to this project being both time consuming and something I enjoy. I am very easily distractible when it comes to math, because I do not enjoy it. | I noticed my longest study session lengths are when I’m writing essays, which makes sense because I become locked in when I’m writing. On the weekends I tend to do less studying because I spend time with my boyfriend.

Data Analyzed: bed time, wakeup time, sleep duration | The bedtime standard deviation is lower than the other numbers, meaning I go to bed a similar time every night. The wakeup time and total sleep have high standard deviations, meaning they’re highly variable (some nights I wake up late and sleep more, some nights I wake up early and sleep less). Wakeup time is positively correlated with sleep duration, which follows this trend. There is no correlation between sleep duration and study time, meaning sleep has little effect on my study time. This is interesting because I would have assumed that I sleep more when I don’t stay up late doing homework. However, if I’m not staying up late doing homework, I’ll realistically spend it on my phone rather than giving myself much needed rest. I wonder how I could mitigate this in the future.

Hypothesis: Increased nightly sleep duration is positively associated with focused study time in the next day. This relationship will be examined while controlling the screen time before bed, nap duration during the day, and daily stress level. | Based on this week’s data, there appears to be a positive relationship between my sleep duration and study focus duration. On the days where I got longer sleep (around 7-8 hours), my study time was consistently higher, reaching up to 6-7 hours. In contrast, on the days where I got shorter sleep (around 6-6.3 hours), my study duration decreased to around 3-4 hours. Overall, the trend in the line graph clearly suggests that increased sleep is associated with increased study productivity/duration in the next day. From this, I learned that my productivity is more affected by sleep than I expected. I used to think I function the same on slightly less sleep but the data shows even losing about an hour of sleep noticeably reduced how long I was able to focus. One barrier I encountered was that there were some days where I studied for several hours but I did not feel very efficient, and there were some days where I studied fewer hours but I got more work done. For next week, I plan to aim for at least 7 hours of sleep each night and try to reduce phone usage before bed to sleep earlier and continuing observing if there is any association.

Sleep: wake up time, bedtime, sleep duration Wake up time and bedtime are positively strongly correlated with a correlation coefficient of .61. This means that the earlier I go to bed, the earlier i wake up (and vice versa). My bedtime is pretty regular, but my wakeup time and my sleep duration are both highly irregular. My wakeup time and sleep duration seem to be positively correlated with earlier wake up time corresponded to less sleep (which makes sense with the high irregularity of wakeup time and sleep duration, yet a routine bedtime). Lastly, I’ve included a graph of my total sleep duration which averages between 7-9 hours. In the future, i will distinguish between waking up from an alarm/ someone else/ noise/ light (external factors) and waking up naturally, to see if there is an association with my sleep duration. A barrier is that remembering exactly when I fall asleep.
